stem: A surveyor stands 60 feet from the base of a building. From that point, the angle of elevation to the top of a radio antenna mounted on the roof is 53 degrees, and the angle of elevation to the roofline (the base of the antenna) is 37 degrees. Given tan 53° = 4/3 and tan 37° = 3/4, what is the height of the radio antenna, in feet?
explanation: Using the tangent ratio with the angle of elevation to the antenna top: height to antenna top = 60 × tan 53° = 60 × (4/3) = 80 feet. Using the tangent ratio with the angle of elevation to the roofline: height to roofline = 60 × tan 37° = 60 × (3/4) = 45 feet. The antenna height is the difference: 80 - 45 = 35 feet.

distractorRationale.A: Computes the total height to the antenna top as 60 × (4/3) = 80, then subtracts the horizontal distance instead of the roofline height: 80 - 60 = 20
distractorRationale.B: Computes only the roofline height using tan 37° and reports it as the antenna height: 60 × (3/4) = 45
distractorRationale.C: Correct: Computes height to antenna top as 60 × (4/3) = 80 and roofline height as 60 × (3/4) = 45, then subtracts to find the antenna height: 80 - 45 = 35
distractorRationale.D: Computes the total height from the ground to the antenna top using tan 53° but does not subtract the roofline height: 60 × (4/3) = 80

imageDescription: A right-triangle diagram showing a surveyor standing 60 feet from the base of a building. Two dashed lines extend from the surveyor's position: one to the roofline (labeled 37°) and one to the top of the antenna on the roof (labeled 53°). The horizontal distance of 60 ft is marked along the ground. The building height to the roofline and the antenna height above the roofline are both marked, with the antenna height labeled with a question mark.
